reasoning: The historical base rate for pandemics is roughly 4-5% per year (e.g., 5 pandemics in the last 100 years: 1918, 1957, 1968, 2009, 2020). While there are ongoing concerns with H5N1 avian influenza spreading among mammals and cattle in the US, as well as the mpox PHEIC, the probability of a new disease making the leap to efficient human-to-human transmission AND being officially characterized as a "pandemic" by the WHO in a single calendar year (2025) remains low. Bureaucratic hesitance to use the term "pandemic" (which is not a formal International Health Regulations tier, unlike a PHEIC) following the political fallout of COVID-19 might also slightly depress the odds. Therefore, remaining closely anchored to the market consensus of 4.2% is well-calibrated and accounts for the background risk securely.
